content.body: Justin Ridgewell can I take over https://github.com/tc39/proposal-private-declarations/
@littledan was interested in the design space for adding privates to objects. He wanted to change the syntax for reusing the outer private in a class/object declaration
yeah I want it to be `[#x]`
content.formatted_body: yeah I want it to be [#x]

content.body: I think that's pretty natural now that we have `#x in foo`
content.formatted_body: I think that's pretty natural now that we have #x in foo

content.body: That's what I suggested. Dan was leaning towards `(#x) = …`
content.formatted_body: That's what I suggested. Dan was leaning towards (#x) = …

content.body: If we'd done `(#x) in foo` I could see it
content.formatted_body: If we'd done (#x) in foo I could see it

content.body: but `#x in foo` already introduces the notion of `#x` in expression position so I think we should just roll with that
content.formatted_body: but #x in foo already introduces the notion of #x in expression position so I think we should just roll with that

content.body: Dan's concern was to have a uniform syntax for both. I don't remember exactly why `[#x]` wouldn't work.
content.formatted_body: Dan's concern was to have a uniform syntax for both. I don't remember exactly why [#x] wouldn't work.

content.body: * Dan's concern was to have a uniform syntax for both classes and objects. I don't remember exactly why `[#x]` wouldn't work.

content.body: Off the top of my head, I think it could work?

content.formatted_body:
there's this one horrible edge case where you can today already write

class A{
  #x;
  prop = foo
  [#x in y]
}

(which is parsed as prop = foo[#x in y])

but that happens whenever you have any computed field, not just with this new form
